meta.title: SF-SPAA-3: Storage, Compute Optimization and Cost Management

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to view all existing users in the account along with their default roles and the date they last logged in. Which command provides this information?

options.text: SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS;
SHOW USERS;
SELECT * FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.USERS;
DESCRIBE ALL USERS;

questions.explanation:
SHOW USERS displays all users with their properties including login_name, display_name, default_role, default_warehouse, last_success_login, and other metadata. ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS is also valid and provides historical/queryable data with SQL, but SHOW USERS is the direct DDL command that provides this information. INFORMATION_SCHEMA does not have a USERS view. DESCRIBE ALL USERS is not valid Snowflake syntax.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: A company's security policy requires that all Snowflake passwords be at least 16 characters, contain at least 2 uppercase letters, 2 lowercase letters, 2 numbers, and 2 special characters, and not match any of the last 5 passwords. Which CREATE PASSWORD POLICY statement implements these requirements?

options.text: CREATE PASSWORD POLICY corp_pw_policy PASSWORD_MIN_LENGTH = 16 PASSWORD_MIN_UPPER_CASE_CHARS = 2 PASSWORD_MIN_LOWER_CASE_CHARS = 2 PASSWORD_MIN_NUMERIC_CHARS = 2 PASSWORD_MIN_SPECIAL_CHARS = 2 PASSWORD_HISTORY = 5;
CREATE PASSWORD POLICY corp_pw_policy MIN_LENGTH = 16 COMPLEXITY = (UPPER=2, LOWER=2, NUMERIC=2, SPECIAL=2) HISTORY = 5;
CREATE PASSWORD POLICY corp_pw_policy WITH (MIN_LENGTH = 16, MIN_UPPERCASE = 2, MIN_LOWERCASE = 2, MIN_DIGITS = 2, MIN_SPECIAL = 2, MAX_REUSE = 5);
CREATE PASSWORD POLICY corp_pw_policy ENFORCE MIN_LENGTH 16 COMPLEXITY UPPER 2 LOWER 2 NUMERIC 2 SPECIAL 2 NO_REUSE 5;

questions.explanation:
The correct Snowflake PASSWORD POLICY parameters use the full prefixed names: PASSWORD_MIN_LENGTH, PASSWORD_MIN_UPPER_CASE_CHARS, PASSWORD_MIN_LOWER_CASE_CHARS, PASSWORD_MIN_NUMERIC_CHARS, PASSWORD_MIN_SPECIAL_CHARS, and PASSWORD_HISTORY. Options B, C, and D use incorrect parameter names that are not valid in Snowflake's PASSWORD POLICY syntax.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is auditing the account's role hierarchy and discovers a custom role FINANCE_ADMIN that was created but never added to the standard hierarchy. Select TWO consequences of this configuration gap. (Select TWO)

options.text: Objects created by roles under FINANCE_ADMIN are not visible to SYSADMIN through normal role hierarchy inheritance
Users cannot be granted the FINANCE_ADMIN role until it is added to the hierarchy
ACCOUNTADMIN cannot view or manage objects owned by FINANCE_ADMIN
Only ACCOUNTADMIN can manage users and objects associated with FINANCE_ADMIN because it is outside the normal hierarchy

questions.explanation:
When a custom role is not granted to SYSADMIN, objects owned by that role (or roles beneath it) are not accessible to SYSADMIN through hierarchy inheritance. SYSADMIN cannot manage, drop, or modify those objects. However, ACCOUNTADMIN inherits ALL roles including orphaned ones, so ACCOUNTADMIN retains visibility and management capability (option **D** is correct).

Option **B** is false — users can still be granted any role regardless of hierarchy position.

Option **C** is false — ACCOUNTADMIN always has full access.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A Snowflake account is on Enterprise edition. An administrator sets the account-level MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 14. A table owner sets DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 90 on a specific table. What is the effective Time Travel retention for that table?

options.text: 14 days, because MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS caps the retention at 14 days
90 days, because the table-level setting overrides the account-level MAX setting
90 days, because MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS controls Fail-Safe extension, not Time Travel retention
7 days, which is the Enterprise edition maximum for Time Travel

questions.explanation:
MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS controls how long Snowflake can extend data retention beyond the set TIME_TRAVEL period when needed by Streams consuming the table — it is not a cap on DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS itself. DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS on the table is independently set to 90 days and that is the effective Time Travel retention. On Enterprise edition, the maximum Time Travel retention is 90 days. option **A** misunderstands what MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS does.

Option **D** is wrong — Enterprise supports up to 90 days.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: A company uses Snowflake at the Organization level with multiple accounts (PROD, DEV, STAGING). An administrator needs to move a database from the DEV account to the PROD account. Which approach is supported natively in Snowflake?

options.text: Use ALTER DATABASE dev_db TRANSFER TO ACCOUNT prod_account;
Use database replication (CREATE DATABASE prod_db AS REPLICA OF dev_account.dev_db) to create a replica in PROD, then promote it
Use the ORGADMIN role to run MOVE DATABASE dev_db TO ACCOUNT prod_account;
Export all tables to an external stage, then import into the PROD account

questions.explanation:
Snowflake's database replication and failover feature allows creating a replica of a database in another account within the same organization. The workflow is: enable replication on the source account, create a replicated database in the target account, refresh the replica, and (optionally) promote it to a primary. ALTER DATABASE TRANSFER and MOVE DATABASE are not valid Snowflake commands. The external stage export/import (**D**) works but is not a native first-class database move feature — it's a manual data migration. Database replication is the intended native approach.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/native-apps-about

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to configure the account so that all users are required to authenticate using SSO (SAML 2.0) through their corporate identity provider, with no password-based login allowed. Which steps are required?

options.text: Configure a SAML integration, set the default_login to SSO, and run ALTER ACCOUNT SET ALLOW_PASSWORD_AUTHENTICATION = FALSE
Create a SECURITY INTEGRATION of type SAML2, configure the IdP, and optionally disable native password auth via a LOGIN POLICY with ALLOW_PASSWORD_AUTHENTICATION = FALSE
Run CREATE SAML PROVIDER, then run ALTER ALL USERS SET AUTHENTICATION = SSO_ONLY
Enable SSO in the Snowflake UI settings and set REQUIRE_SSO = TRUE in a session policy

questions.explanation:
SSO via SAML2 in Snowflake requires creating a SECURITY INTEGRATION (CREATE SECURITY INTEGRATION ... TYPE = SAML2) configured with the identity provider's metadata. To prevent password-based logins, a LOGIN POLICY can be configured with ALLOW_PASSWORD_AUTHENTICATION = FALSE and applied at the account or user level. The exact parameter name may vary — the key concepts are: SAML2 Security Integration for SSO setup, and LOGIN POLICY to restrict authentication methods. option **A** uses ALTER ACCOUNT SET with incorrect parameters. option **C** uses invalid syntax. option **D** describes an invalid session policy attribute.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: An administrator is setting up Snowflake for a new team and needs to create a proper role and object structure. The team needs a custom role DATASCIENCE_ROLE that will own a database DS_DB. Select TWO commands that correctly establish the intended ownership structure. (Select TWO)

options.text: USE ROLE SYSADMIN; CREATE ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE; GRANT ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE TO ROLE SYSADMIN;
USE ROLE SYSADMIN; CREATE DATABASE DS_DB; GRANT OWNERSHIP ON DATABASE DS_DB TO ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE;
USE ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE; CREATE DATABASE DS_DB; (This creates the database owned by DATASCIENCE_ROLE)
USE ROLE SECURITYADMIN; CREATE ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE; GRANT ROLE DATASCIENCE_ROLE TO ROLE SYSADMIN;

questions.explanation:
Creating the database while using DATASCIENCE_ROLE (**C**) automatically sets DATASCIENCE_ROLE as the owner — the creating role becomes the owner in Snowflake. Creating the role under SECURITYADMIN (**D**) and granting it to SYSADMIN follows best practice role management (SECURITYADMIN is the appropriate role for creating roles and users; SYSADMIN should not be used for role creation ideally). option **A** creates the role using SYSADMIN — technically possible but SECURITYADMIN is more appropriate for role management. option **B** (SYSADMIN creates the DB then grants ownership) also works but transfers ownership, which is a less direct approach and SYSADMIN would briefly own the DB.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A company needs to restrict the maximum duration that any individual SQL statement can run, regardless of the warehouse. Which account parameter controls this?

options.text: LOCK_TIMEOUT
QUERY_TAG
STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS
SESSION_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS

questions.explanation:
STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS sets the maximum number of seconds a SQL statement can run before it is automatically cancelled. It can be set at the account, warehouse, or session level, with more specific levels overriding broader ones. LOCK_TIMEOUT controls how long a query waits to acquire a lock (not total execution time). QUERY_TAG is a label for tracking queries. SESSION_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS is not a standard Snowflake account parameter — idle session timeout is managed through session policies.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to set up a resource monitor that sends a notification email when 80% of the monthly quota is consumed, suspends the warehouse when 100% is reached, but does NOT want to create a new trigger for every 10% above 100%. Which trigger configuration is needed?

options.text: TRIGGERS ON 80 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND ON 110 PERCENT DO SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY
TRIGGERS ON 80 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND
TRIGGERS ON 80 PERCENT DO ALERT ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND
TRIGGERS ON 80 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 100 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 110 PERCENT DO SUSPEND

questions.explanation:
The requirement is exactly two triggers: NOTIFY at 80% and SUSPEND at 100%. SUSPEND allows running queries to finish before suspending the warehouse. There is no requirement for additional triggers above 100%. option **A** adds an unnecessary SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY at 110%. ALERT (**C**) is not a valid trigger action — the valid actions are NOTIFY, SUSPEND, and SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY. option **D** has NOTIFY at 100% which delays the suspension to 110%, contradicting the requirement.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors

questions.stem: A company wants to analyze which virtual warehouses have consumed the most credits over the past 7 days, grouped by hour. Which query provides this information?

options.text: SELECT WAREHOUSE_NAME, DATE_TRUNC('HOUR', START_TIME) AS HOUR, SUM(CREDITS_USED) FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY WHERE START_TIME >= DATEADD('day', -7, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()) GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 3 DESC;
SELECT SERVICE_TYPE, USAGE_DATE, SUM(CREDITS_USED) FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY WHERE USAGE_DATE >= DATEADD('day', -7, CURRENT_DATE()) GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 3 DESC;
SELECT WAREHOUSE_NAME, CREDITS_USED FROM SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY WHERE START_TIME >= DATEADD('hour', -168, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()) ORDER BY CREDITS_USED DESC;
SELECT * FROM TABLE(SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY(DATE_RANGE_START => DATEADD('day', -7, CURRENT_DATE())));

questions.explanation:
ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY provides per-warehouse, per-hour credit consumption. The query groups by warehouse and hour (DATE_TRUNC to the hour) and sums credits over the past 7 days — exactly what is needed. option **B** uses METERING_DAILY_HISTORY which is daily granularity, not hourly, and does not break down by warehouse. option **C** (INFORMATION_SCHEMA) is the table-valued function form with limited retention (14 days) but the syntax is missing the DATE_RANGE_END parameter and the aggregation needed. option **D** uses the INFORMATION_SCHEMA table function form which also works for 14-day lookbacks but does not aggregate by hour.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to alert the finance team when daily credit consumption across all warehouses exceeds 500 credits. Which TWO approaches can provide this capability? (Select TWO)

options.text: Create a resource monitor at the account level with CREDIT_QUOTA = 500 and FREQUENCY = DAILY, trigger action NOTIFY at 100%
Create a Snowflake Alert that queries METERING_DAILY_HISTORY and sends a notification when the daily total exceeds 500
Set ALTER ACCOUNT SET MAX_DAILY_CREDITS = 500 to automatically alert and stop compute when the limit is reached
Create a Snowflake Task that runs daily, queries WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY, and calls SYSTEM$SEND_EMAIL if the threshold is exceeded

questions.explanation:
A daily account-level resource monitor (**A**) is a built-in Snowflake mechanism specifically for credit threshold notifications. Snowflake Alerts (**B**) allow scheduling a condition check query against ACCOUNT_USAGE views and triggering notifications — this provides flexible, custom threshold monitoring. Snowflake Tasks + SYSTEM$SEND_EMAIL (**D**) would technically work as a custom solution but requires more setup; Snowflake Alerts are the more direct built-in feature for this. ALTER ACCOUNT SET MAX_DAILY_CREDITS (**C**) is not a valid Snowflake parameter.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors

questions.stem: An administrator notices that Snowpipe is generating unexpectedly high serverless credit charges. Resource monitors assigned to the production warehouses show healthy consumption. Why are the Snowpipe charges not captured in the warehouse-level resource monitors?

options.text: Snowpipe credits are counted toward whichever warehouse processed the most data that day
Snowpipe uses serverless compute that is separate from virtual warehouses; resource monitors only track virtual warehouse credits, not serverless credits
Snowpipe credits appear in resource monitors only after a 24-hour delay
Snowpipe requires a dedicated warehouse; the administrator needs to create a SNOWPIPE warehouse and assign the monitor to it

questions.explanation:
Snowpipe is a serverless feature — it does not use customer-managed virtual warehouses. Instead, it uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute that is billed separately. Resource monitors only track credits consumed by virtual warehouses. Serverless credits (Snowpipe, automatic clustering, materialized view maintenance, search optimization, Snowpark Container Services) are not captured by resource monitors. To monitor these, administrators must use METERING_DAILY_HISTORY filtered by SERVICE_TYPE = 'SNOWPIPE', or use the BUDGET feature. Options A, C, and **D** are all incorrect — Snowpipe has its own serverless billing pathway.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/search-optimization-service

questions.stem: A resource monitor with CREDIT_QUOTA = 1000 and FREQUENCY = MONTHLY is assigned to both the ANALYTICS_WH and REPORTING_WH. After 15 days, ANALYTICS_WH has consumed 700 credits and REPORTING_WH has consumed 200 credits. The NOTIFY trigger is set at 80%. What is the current status?

options.text: No notification triggered yet; each warehouse is evaluated independently (700/1000 = 70% for ANALYTICS_WH, 200/1000 = 20% for REPORTING_WH)
Notification triggered; the combined consumption is 900 credits (900/1000 = 90%), which exceeds the 80% threshold
Two separate notifications triggered, one for each warehouse as they are tracked independently
No notification triggered; resource monitors only count credits from the warehouse that consumes the most

questions.explanation:
When a single resource monitor is assigned to multiple warehouses, credits consumed by ALL assigned warehouses are pooled and counted toward the single shared quota. ANALYTICS_WH (700) + REPORTING_WH (200) = 900 credits total. 900/1000 = 90%, which has already exceeded the 80% NOTIFY threshold. A notification email should have been sent. The warehouses are not tracked independently against separate quotas — they share the same 1000-credit pool. option **A** incorrectly treats them independently. option **C** implies separate tracking.

Option **D** is incorrect.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors

questions.stem: A new table is created by ROLE_A in the ANALYTICS schema. ROLE_B needs to query this table. What is the MINIMUM set of privileges ROLE_B requires?

options.text: SELECT on the table only
USAGE on the database, USAGE on the schema, and SELECT on the table
USAGE on the database and SELECT on the table
OWNERSHIP on the table

questions.explanation:
Snowflake requires a role to have USAGE at every level of the object hierarchy to access an object. To query a table: (1) USAGE on the database to access it, (2) USAGE on the schema to see into it, and (3) SELECT on the table to read its rows. Missing any one of these results in an 'object does not exist or access denied' error. SELECT alone (**A**) is not sufficient without the container USAGEs. USAGE on database without schema USAGE (**C**) is also insufficient. OWNERSHIP (**D**) is far more than minimum necessary.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to grant a role the ability to create objects in a schema WITHOUT giving the role full ownership of the schema. Which privilege achieves this?

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA myschema TO ROLE builder_role;
GRANT CREATE TABLE ON SCHEMA myschema TO ROLE builder_role;
GRANT MODIFY ON SCHEMA myschema TO ROLE builder_role;
GRANT OWNERSHIP ON SCHEMA myschema TO ROLE builder_role;

questions.explanation:
GRANT CREATE TABLE ON SCHEMA (or CREATE VIEW, CREATE STAGE, CREATE FUNCTION, etc.) grants the ability to create that specific type of object within the schema without granting schema ownership. Multiple CREATE privileges can be granted to give broader object creation capability. GRANT USAGE only allows navigating into the schema and accessing objects, not creating new ones. GRANT MODIFY on a schema changes schema-level properties, not object creation rights. GRANT OWNERSHIP transfers full control of the schema, which is excessive.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-load-local-file-system-create-stage

questions.stem: A security team requires that all PII columns in the database are masked for users without the COMPLIANCE_ROLE. The masking policy should be applied to 50 tables across 5 schemas. Select TWO efficient approaches to apply the masking policy at scale. (Select TWO)

options.text: Write a stored procedure that queries INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS for columns with a naming convention (e.g., ending in '_PII') and programmatically applies the masking policy using ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN SET MASKING POLICY
Use a TAG-based masking policy: create a tag (e.g., PII_TAG), apply the tag to all PII columns, and attach the masking policy to the tag using ALTER TAG SET MASKING POLICY
Create one masking policy per table and apply each individually using separate ALTER TABLE statements for each column
Grant the masking policy at the schema level to automatically apply to all current and future columns

questions.explanation:
The stored procedure approach (**A**) allows programmatic, scalable application based on column naming conventions — one script applies policies across all tables. Tag-based masking (**B**) is the most scalable approach: attach a tag like 'PII' to any column, and the masking policy automatically applies to all columns with that tag — including future columns when the tag is applied. Creating one policy per table (**C**) is both tedious and does not scale. There is no schema-level masking policy grant that auto-applies to all columns (option **D** is not a valid Snowflake feature).

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-intro

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator runs the following command: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN DATABASE mydb TO ROLE reporting_role; A new table is created in mydb three days later. Does reporting_role have SELECT on the new table?

options.text: Yes, because the GRANT ON ALL TABLES grant applies to all current and future tables
No, because GRANT ON ALL TABLES is a one-time bulk grant to existing tables only; it does not create a future grant
Yes, but only if the new table is created by SYSADMIN or a role reporting_role can inherit from
No, because bulk grants at the database level are not supported in Snowflake

questions.explanation:
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN DATABASE is a bulk one-time grant that applies to all tables that exist at the time the command is run. It does NOT create a future grant. Tables created after this command will NOT automatically receive the SELECT grant for reporting_role. To handle future tables, a separate GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN DATABASE mydb TO ROLE reporting_role must also be executed. This is a common Snowflake gotcha — 'ALL TABLES' is retrospective only; 'FUTURE TABLES' is prospective.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: An administrator creates a row access policy that restricts data based on the user's assigned regions in a mapping table. During testing, ACCOUNTADMIN queries the table and sees no rows. Select TWO reasons this behavior is expected and what should be done. (Select TWO)

options.text: Row access policies apply to all roles including ACCOUNTADMIN; ACCOUNTADMIN is not exempt
ACCOUNTADMIN's username is likely not in the region_mapping table, so the policy returns FALSE for all rows
The fix is to add an explicit check in the policy: CURRENT_ROLE() = 'ACCOUNTADMIN' OR (mapping table condition)
The fix is to run ALTER TABLE ... ALTER ROW ACCESS POLICY BYPASS ROLES = ('ACCOUNTADMIN')

questions.explanation:
Row access policies in Snowflake apply universally — including to ACCOUNTADMIN. There is no automatic bypass. The behavior seen (zero rows) is expected when ACCOUNTADMIN is not in the mapping table, because the policy evaluates to FALSE for every row. The correct fix (**C**) is to add explicit privileged role bypass logic in the policy definition itself, e.g.: CASE WHEN CURRENT_ROLE() = 'ACCOUNTADMIN' THEN TRUE ELSE EXISTS(mapping table lookup) END.

Option **B** is correct as a diagnosis but not listed as a fix action. ALTER TABLE ... BYPASS ROLES (**D**) is not valid Snowflake syntax for row access policies.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-row-intro

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to verify which network policy is currently applied to the account. Which command provides this information?

options.text: SHOW NETWORK POLICIES;
SELECT CURRENT_NETWORK_POLICY();
DESCRIBE ACCOUNT;
SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'NETWORK_POLICY' IN ACCOUNT;

questions.explanation:
SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'NETWORK_POLICY' IN ACCOUNT shows whether a network policy is set at the account level and which policy it is. This is the direct way to check the account-level network policy parameter. SHOW NETWORK POLICIES lists all defined network policies but does not show which one is applied at the account level. SELECT CURRENT_NETWORK_POLICY() is not a valid Snowflake function. DESCRIBE ACCOUNT shows account metadata but not parameter settings.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator notices that a network policy blocks connections from a specific IP that a vendor needs to use. The account policy is managed by a different team and cannot be changed quickly. The vendor's user account is VENDOR_USER. What is the fastest solution to allow the vendor access?

options.text: Create a new network policy that includes the vendor's IP and assign it to VENDOR_USER using ALTER USER VENDOR_USER SET NETWORK_POLICY = vendor_policy
Ask the vendor to use a VPN to access Snowflake from an approved IP range
Temporarily disable the account network policy using ALTER ACCOUNT UNSET NETWORK_POLICY
Add the vendor's IP to the ALLOWED_IP_LIST on the existing account network policy

questions.explanation:
A user-level network policy overrides the account-level network policy for that specific user. Creating a vendor-specific policy that includes the vendor's IP and assigning it directly to VENDOR_USER is the fastest targeted solution that does not affect other users or require changing the account-level policy. Options B and D require coordination with other teams or the vendor. option **C** removes protection for all users, which is a serious security risk.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: A company using Azure is implementing Azure Private Link for Snowflake. Select TWO accurate statements about this implementation. (Select TWO)

options.text: Azure Private Link for Snowflake requires Business Critical edition or higher
After enabling Private Link, the public internet endpoint for the Snowflake account is automatically disabled
Clients must update their connection strings to use the Private Link-specific hostname
Azure Private Link works across different Azure regions — the VNET and Snowflake account do not need to be in the same region

questions.explanation:
Azure Private Link for Snowflake is only available on Business Critical edition (option **A** is true). Clients must use the Private Link-specific hostname (which includes 'privatelink' in the URL) — the standard public endpoint URL routes through the public internet, while the Private Link endpoint routes through Azure's private network backbone (option **C** is true). The public endpoint is NOT automatically disabled when Private Link is enabled — both can coexist; administrators must explicitly enforce Private Link-only access if needed (option **B** is false). Azure Private Link requires the VNET and the Snowflake account to be in the same Azure region (option **D** is false).

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is setting up external access integration for a Python UDF that needs to call an external REST API. The company's security policy requires that only specific external domains be allowed. What is the correct Snowflake construct to enforce this?

options.text: Create a network policy with ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('<api_domain_ip>') and assign it to the UDF's owner role
Create a NETWORK RULE with TYPE = HOST and VALUE_LIST = ('api.example.com'), then include it in an EXTERNAL ACCESS INTEGRATION
Set ALTER ACCOUNT SET ALLOWED_EXTERNAL_DOMAINS = ('api.example.com')
Create a network policy with TYPE = EGRESS and add the domain to ALLOWED_DOMAINS

questions.explanation:
For controlling egress from Snowflake UDFs and stored procedures to external services, the correct approach uses Network Rules (TYPE = HOST for domain-based rules) combined with External Access Integrations. A NETWORK RULE with TYPE = HOST specifies allowed external hostnames. The EXTERNAL ACCESS INTEGRATION object ties together the network rule and optional secrets, and is then referenced in the UDF/stored procedure definition. Network policies (options A and D) control inbound access to Snowflake, not outbound egress from UDFs. ALTER ACCOUNT SET ALLOWED_EXTERNAL_DOMAINS (**C**) is not a valid Snowflake parameter.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: A warehouse is configured with SIZE = 'X-LARGE', AUTO_SUSPEND = 300, and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE. If the warehouse has been idle for 6 minutes (360 seconds), what is its current state?

options.text: Running but idle, because auto-suspend has not yet triggered
Suspended, because 360 seconds of idle time exceeds the AUTO_SUSPEND threshold of 300 seconds
Running, because X-LARGE warehouses have a minimum runtime of 10 minutes before auto-suspend
Suspended, but it will automatically resume every 30 minutes to check for queued queries

questions.explanation:
AUTO_SUSPEND = 300 means the warehouse suspends after 300 seconds (5 minutes) of inactivity. With 360 seconds (6 minutes) of idle time, the threshold has been exceeded and the warehouse is suspended. Warehouse size (X-LARGE) does not affect the auto-suspend timing — it operates the same way regardless of size. Suspended warehouses do not periodically wake up to check for queries — they remain suspended until a query is submitted (with AUTO_RESUME = TRUE).

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to enable the Query Acceleration Service for a specific warehouse to help with ad-hoc analytical queries that occasionally scan very large datasets. The service should not scale beyond 4x the warehouse's compute for any single query. Which command configures this?

options.text: ALTER WAREHOUSE analytics_wh SET ENABLE_QUERY_ACCELERATION = TRUE QUERY_ACCELERATION_MAX_SCALE_FACTOR = 4;
ALTER WAREHOUSE analytics_wh SET QUERY_ACCELERATION = ENABLED MAX_SCALE = 4;
CREATE QUERY ACCELERATION POLICY qa_policy MAX_SCALE = 4; ALTER WAREHOUSE analytics_wh SET QUERY_ACCELERATION_POLICY = qa_policy;
ALTER WAREHOUSE analytics_wh SET ENABLE_QUERY_ACCELERATION = TRUE; ALTER ACCOUNT SET QUERY_ACCELERATION_MAX_SCALE_FACTOR = 4;

questions.explanation:
The correct Snowflake syntax to enable Query Acceleration Service uses ENABLE_QUERY_ACCELERATION = TRUE and optionally QUERY_ACCELERATION_MAX_SCALE_FACTOR to cap the scale factor (how much additional serverless compute can be added relative to the warehouse size). The default MAX_SCALE_FACTOR is 8 if not specified. Both parameters can be set in a single ALTER WAREHOUSE statement. option **B** uses incorrect parameter names. option **C** invents a non-existent QUERY ACCELERATION POLICY object. option **D** incorrectly tries to set the scale factor at the account level rather than the warehouse level.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/query-acceleration-service

questions.stem: A DBA is evaluating the storage costs for a Snowflake account on Enterprise edition. Select TWO true statements about how storage is billed in Snowflake. (Select TWO)

options.text: Time Travel storage (historical versions of modified or deleted data) is billed at the same rate as active storage
Fail-Safe storage (the 7-day Snowflake-managed protection period) is charged to the customer in addition to Time Travel storage
Reducing DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS on a table immediately frees up Time Travel storage and reduces costs
Cloning a table creates an independent copy of all data immediately, doubling the storage cost for the cloned table

questions.explanation:
Time Travel storage is billed at the same per-TB rate as active storage (option **A** is true). Fail-Safe provides an additional 7 days of data protection managed by Snowflake and is also billed to the customer (option **B** is true) — total protected storage can be active + Time Travel + Fail-Safe. Reducing DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS does not immediately purge historical versions — Snowflake's background processes eventually clean up, but it is not instant (option **C** is false). Table cloning in Snowflake uses zero-copy cloning — no data is physically copied at clone time, so storage cost is not doubled immediately; only divergent (changed) data incurs additional storage (option **D** is false).

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: An administrator is enabling automatic clustering on a very large table (10 TB) with a cluster key of (REGION, ORDER_DATE). The table receives heavy INSERT activity throughout the day. What ongoing cost consideration should the administrator communicate to stakeholders?

options.text: Automatic clustering is free because it uses idle warehouse compute cycles
Automatic clustering consumes serverless credits continuously as Snowflake reclusters micro-partitions in the background, charged separately from warehouse credits
Automatic clustering charges a one-time fee at the time the CLUSTER BY key is defined
Automatic clustering uses the warehouse's existing compute and is included in the warehouse's credit consumption

questions.explanation:
Automatic clustering is a serverless feature — it uses Snowflake-managed background compute, not the customer's virtual warehouses. Credits are charged continuously as the service reclusters micro-partitions. For a large, heavily-modified table, these charges can be significant. Administrators should monitor ACCOUNT_USAGE.AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY to track credit consumption. There is no one-time fee. It is not free, and it does not use existing warehouse compute (it is completely separate from virtual warehouse billing).

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions

questions.stem: A CISO requests a report of all queries that accessed the PATIENTS table containing PHI data in the last 6 months, including the user, timestamp, and specific columns accessed. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view and approach best satisfies this request?

options.text: Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY WHERE QUERY_TEXT ILIKE '%PATIENTS%' to find queries referencing the table
Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY WHERE DIRECT_OBJECTS_ACCESSED LIKE '%PATIENTS%' to get structured access records with column-level detail
Query SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY for the past 6 months of query logs
Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY to see all users who logged in during that period

questions.explanation:
ACCESS_HISTORY is the correct view for PHI access auditing. It contains structured records of which objects (tables, views) and columns were accessed by each query, captured by user, role, timestamp, and query ID. DIRECT_OBJECTS_ACCESSED and BASE_OBJECTS_ACCESSED are semi-structured columns (arrays of objects) that contain table and column names. This provides the precise column-level audit trail needed for PHI compliance. QUERY_HISTORY text search (**A**) is unreliable — queries might use aliases, views, or dynamic SQL. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY only has 7-day retention (**C**). LOGIN_HISTORY tracks authentication, not data access (**D**).

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/semistructured-intro

questions.stem: A security team needs to identify all privilege grants that occurred in the past 30 days, including who granted them and to whom. INFORMATION_SCHEMA does not retain sufficient history. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view and columns provide this information?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES — columns CREATED_ON, PRIVILEGE, GRANTED_ON, NAME, GRANTEE_NAME, GRANTED_BY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_USERS — columns CREATED_ON, ROLE, GRANTEE_NAME, GRANTED_BY
Both GRANTS_TO_ROLES and GRANTS_TO_USERS should be queried to capture all privilege grant activity
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY filtered for QUERY_TYPE = 'GRANT'

questions.explanation:
Privilege grants in Snowflake are of two types: privileges granted to roles (object privileges like SELECT, INSERT, USAGE — captured in GRANTS_TO_ROLES) and role grants to users (GRANTS_TO_USERS). To capture ALL grant activity from the past 30 days, both views must be queried. GRANTS_TO_ROLES captures when privileges on objects were granted to roles (including GRANTED_BY to see who issued the grant). GRANTS_TO_USERS captures when roles were granted to users. Both views have 1-year retention. QUERY_HISTORY with QUERY_TYPE = 'GRANT' could work but provides raw SQL without structured privilege metadata.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is configuring data masking for a financial application. The CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER column should show the full number to FRAUD_ANALYST_ROLE and a masked version (showing only last 4 digits) to all other roles. Select TWO valid ways to implement this conditional masking logic in the policy. (Select TWO)

options.text: CASE WHEN CURRENT_ROLE() = 'FRAUD_ANALYST_ROLE' THEN val ELSE CONCAT('XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-', RIGHT(val, 4)) END
IFF(IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION('FRAUD_ANALYST_ROLE'), val, CONCAT('XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-', RIGHT(val, 4)))
CASE WHEN CURRENT_USER() IN (SELECT username FROM fraud_analyst_users) THEN val ELSE 'XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX' END
GRANT UNMASK ON COLUMN credit_card_number TO ROLE FRAUD_ANALYST_ROLE — no masking policy needed

questions.explanation:
option **A** uses CURRENT_ROLE() to check the active primary role and applies conditional masking. This is the standard pattern for role-based masking. option **B** uses IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() which checks if a role is active in the session (including secondary roles), making it broader and useful when users may have the role as either primary or secondary. Both are valid masking policy expressions. option **C** uses a user-based lookup which could work functionally but is not a recommended pattern (user names can change, and this mixes user identity with role-based access control). GRANT UNMASK (**D**) is not a valid Snowflake privilege — masking is controlled entirely through policy logic.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-intro

questions.stem: A regulated financial services company requires that Snowflake activity logs be retained for 7 years for compliance purposes, but ACCOUNT_USAGE only retains data for 1 year. What architectural approach should the administrator implement?

options.text: Contact Snowflake support to extend ACCOUNT_USAGE retention to 7 years for an additional fee
Create Snowflake Tasks that periodically query ACCOUNT_USAGE views and INSERT results into customer-managed tables (compliance archive tables) with no time limit
Enable the Data Retention Extension feature in account settings to extend ACCOUNT_USAGE to 7 years
Use Snowflake's built-in compliance mode which automatically retains all audit logs for 7 years

questions.explanation:
ACCOUNT_USAGE views have a fixed 1-year retention that cannot be extended — Snowflake does not offer extended retention for these system views regardless of support requests (option **A** is false). The standard architectural pattern for long-term audit log retention is to use Snowflake Tasks to regularly extract ACCOUNT_USAGE data (LOGIN_HISTORY, QUERY_HISTORY, ACCESS_HISTORY, GRANTS_TO_ROLES, etc.) and insert it into customer-managed tables within the account (or export to external storage). Customer-managed tables have configurable retention. There is no Data Retention Extension feature for ACCOUNT_USAGE (option **C** is false). Snowflake does not have a built-in 7-year compliance mode (option **D** is false).

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: An administrator wants to set a hard limit on how long any transaction can hold locks on resources in the account, to prevent long-running uncommitted transactions from blocking other operations. Which parameter should be configured at the account level?

options.text: STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS
TRANSACTION_ABORT_ON_ERROR
LOCK_TIMEOUT
QUERY_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS

questions.explanation:
LOCK_TIMEOUT controls the maximum number of seconds a statement will wait to acquire a lock before returning an error. Setting this at the account level establishes an account-wide default preventing indefinite lock waits that can cascade into blocking. STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS limits total query execution time, not specifically lock wait time. TRANSACTION_ABORT_ON_ERROR controls whether a transaction rolls back on statement errors. QUERY_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS is not a standard Snowflake account parameter name.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: An administrator wants to verify whether the STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS parameter has been explicitly set at the account level or is using the system default. Which command provides this distinction?

options.text: SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'STATEMENT_TIMEOUT%' IN ACCOUNT;
SELECT CURRENT_PARAMETERS();
DESCRIBE PARAMETER STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS;
SHOW ACCOUNT PARAMETERS CURRENT;

questions.explanation:
SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'STATEMENT_TIMEOUT%' IN ACCOUNT returns rows for matching parameters, including columns for the current value, the default value, the type, and critically a 'level' column that shows whether the parameter was set at the ACCOUNT level or is the SYSTEM DEFAULT. This distinction tells the administrator whether an explicit account-level setting exists. SELECT CURRENT_PARAMETERS() is not a valid Snowflake function. DESCRIBE PARAMETER and SHOW ACCOUNT PARAMETERS CURRENT are not valid Snowflake syntax.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to unlock a service account that was locked after repeated failed key pair authentication attempts. Which command accomplishes this without enabling password login?

options.text: ALTER USER svc_account RESET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY;
ALTER USER svc_account SET MINS_TO_UNLOCK = 0;
ALTER USER svc_account SET DISABLED = FALSE;
DROP USER svc_account; CREATE USER svc_account RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<key>';

questions.explanation:
ALTER USER ... SET MINS_TO_UNLOCK = 0 immediately clears the lockout state for any user, including service accounts using key pair authentication. This does not change the authentication method or enable password login. DISABLED = FALSE re-enables a manually disabled user account, not a login-locked account. RESET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY is not valid Snowflake syntax. Dropping and recreating the user would also unlock it but would lose all grants and role memberships associated with the account.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: A new data engineer joins the team. The administrator needs to configure their account so that upon login they automatically use the DATA_ENGINEER_ROLE role and the DE_WH warehouse, and their default database/schema context is set to PROD_DB.STAGING. Select TWO properties that configure this experience. (Select TWO)

options.text: DEFAULT_ROLE = 'DATA_ENGINEER_ROLE' and DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE = 'DE_WH'
DEFAULT_NAMESPACE = 'PROD_DB.STAGING' — sets the default database and schema context
SESSION_ROLE = 'DATA_ENGINEER_ROLE' and SESSION_WAREHOUSE = 'DE_WH'
DEFAULT_DATABASE = 'PROD_DB' and DEFAULT_SCHEMA = 'STAGING'

questions.explanation:
DEFAULT_ROLE and DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE are the correct Snowflake user properties for setting the automatic role and warehouse at session start. DEFAULT_NAMESPACE = 'DATABASE.SCHEMA' sets the default database and schema context — this is the correct property name for the combined namespace. SESSION_ROLE and SESSION_WAREHOUSE are not valid Snowflake user properties. While setting DEFAULT_DATABASE and DEFAULT_SCHEMA separately (**D**) might seem logical, the correct Snowflake property for the combined database.schema context is DEFAULT_NAMESPACE.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: A Snowflake governance team is using role COMMENT fields to document ownership. They need to update the comment on SALES_DATA_ROLE to reflect the new owning team. The role currently has comment 'Old Sales Team'. What command updates it?

options.text: UPDATE ROLE sales_data_role SET COMMENT = 'New Sales Operations Team';
ALTER ROLE sales_data_role SET COMMENT = 'New Sales Operations Team';
MODIFY ROLE sales_data_role COMMENT = 'New Sales Operations Team';
CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE sales_data_role COMMENT = 'New Sales Operations Team';

questions.explanation:
ALTER ROLE role_name SET COMMENT = 'text' is the correct Snowflake command to add or update the comment on an existing role. The comment is visible in SHOW ROLES output and through the Snowflake web UI for governance documentation. UPDATE ROLE and MODIFY ROLE are not valid Snowflake DDL syntax. CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE would drop and recreate the role, destroying all role grants and memberships — highly destructive for a comment update.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: Multiple Snowflake tasks call the same stored procedure. Inside the procedure, different cleanup logic should run depending on which task invoked it. Which function enables this task-aware branching?

options.text: CURRENT_STATEMENT()
SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME()
SESSION_CONTEXT('task')
GET_TASK_ID()

questions.explanation:
SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME() returns the fully-qualified name of the Snowflake task that is currently executing the stored procedure. By comparing this value in an IF/CASE statement, the procedure can branch to different logic depending on which task invoked it. CURRENT_STATEMENT() returns the SQL text of the executing statement, not the task name. SESSION_CONTEXT('task') is not a valid Snowflake function. GET_TASK_ID() is not a valid Snowflake system function.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: A Snowflake deployment script needs to create multiple roles without failing if some roles already exist from previous deployments. Which syntax makes each CREATE ROLE statement idempotent?

options.text: CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS role_name;
CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE role_name;
MERGE INTO ROLES VALUES (role_name);
INSERT ROLE IF ABSENT role_name;

questions.explanation:
CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS is the idempotent form that creates the role only if it does not already exist. If the role exists, no error is raised and no changes are made to the existing role or its grants. CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE (**B**) would drop the existing role and create a new one, destroying all grants — unacceptable in an idempotent deployment script. MERGE INTO ROLES and INSERT ROLE are not valid Snowflake syntax.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: An administrator wants to audit all roles in a Snowflake account and filter the results to show only roles whose names contain the string 'DATA'. Which is the correct filtering syntax?

options.text: SHOW ROLES LIKE '%DATA%';
SHOW ROLES CONTAINING 'DATA';
LIST ROLES WHERE NAME CONTAINS 'DATA';
SELECT * FROM ROLES WHERE ROLE_NAME CONTAINS 'DATA';

questions.explanation:
SHOW ROLES LIKE '%DATA%' uses SQL LIKE wildcard pattern matching to filter the results — % matches zero or more characters, so '%DATA%' returns all roles where 'DATA' appears anywhere in the name. SHOW ROLES CONTAINING is not valid Snowflake syntax. LIST ROLES and the SELECT from ROLES (without SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE) are not valid commands. The ACCOUNT_USAGE.ROLES view could be queried with ILIKE '%DATA%' but SHOW ROLES LIKE is the direct DDL approach.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator has been notified that behavior change bundle '2024_07' is scheduled to become mandatory next quarter. They want to proactively test whether any existing queries or procedures break under the new behavior. Which Snowflake function enables the bundle for testing in a dev account?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE_ENABLED = '2024_07';
SELECT SYSTEM$ENABLE_BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE('2024_07');
SET PARAMETER BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE = '2024_07';
CREATE BEHAVIOR CHANGE TRIAL FOR BUNDLE '2024_07';

questions.explanation:
SYSTEM$ENABLE_BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE('bundle_name') is the correct Snowflake system function to opt an account into a specific behavior change bundle before it is automatically enforced. Running this in a dev account allows testing the impact of the upcoming changes on existing workloads. ALTER ACCOUNT SET BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE_ENABLED (**A**) is not valid Snowflake DDL. SET PARAMETER BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE (**C**) is not valid syntax. CREATE BEHAVIOR CHANGE TRIAL (**D**) is not a Snowflake construct.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: A company assigns resource monitors to individual warehouses. Warehouse A has its own resource monitor with CREDIT_QUOTA = 200. Warehouse A also falls under the account-level resource monitor with CREDIT_QUOTA = 5000. When Warehouse A consumes 200 credits, which monitor's trigger fires?

options.text: Only the warehouse-level monitor fires; it takes precedence over the account-level monitor for Warehouse A
Both monitors fire simultaneously when Warehouse A reaches 200 credits
Only the account-level monitor fires because it has a higher quota
Warehouse A's credits count toward both monitors independently

questions.explanation:
When a warehouse has its own resource monitor assigned, that warehouse-level monitor takes precedence and the account-level monitor does not count credits from that warehouse. Warehouse **A** is governed exclusively by its own 200-credit monitor. Credits from warehouses with dedicated monitors are excluded from the account-level monitor's tracking. Only warehouses without their own resource monitor contribute to the account-level monitor's credit count. Both monitors do not fire simultaneously for the same warehouse.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors

questions.stem: A resource monitor was set with NOTIFY_USERS = ('ADMIN_A') and a NOTIFY trigger at 80%. ADMIN_A left the company and their account was dropped. The monitor still has ADMIN_A in NOTIFY_USERS. What happens when 80% is reached?

options.text: The resource monitor is automatically disabled because the NOTIFY_USERS list contains an invalid user
The notification is silently skipped because the user no longer exists; the monitor continues to track credits normally
Snowflake raises an error and the NOTIFY trigger does not fire
All ACCOUNTADMIN users receive the notification instead as a fallback

questions.explanation:
Resource monitor notifications are sent on a best-effort basis. If a user in NOTIFY_USERS no longer exists or has no valid email, the notification for that user is silently skipped. The resource monitor continues to track credits and fire triggers as configured — the monitoring function is not impaired by stale user references. The remaining trigger actions (SUSPEND, SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY) would still fire at their configured thresholds. Administrators should periodically audit NOTIFY_USERS lists when team members change.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: An administrator needs to increase the monthly credit quota on a resource monitor from 500 to 800 credits in the middle of the current monitoring period. Which command achieves this without resetting the usage counter?

options.text: ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR my_monitor SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 800;
DROP RESOURCE MONITOR my_monitor; CREATE RESOURCE MONITOR my_monitor WITH CREDIT_QUOTA = 800;
ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR my_monitor ADD 300 TO CREDIT_QUOTA;
UPDATE RESOURCE MONITOR my_monitor SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 800;

questions.explanation:
ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR my_monitor SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 800 updates the quota value mid-period without resetting the CREDITS_USED counter. The monitor continues tracking from wherever it is in the current period, now measured against the new 800-credit quota. Dropping and recreating (**B**) would reset CREDITS_USED to zero, losing tracking continuity. ADD N TO CREDIT_QUOTA (**C**) and UPDATE RESOURCE MONITOR (**D**) are not valid Snowflake syntax.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/resource-monitors

questions.stem: An administrator wants to identify which virtual warehouses are experiencing the most query queuing during business hours. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides warehouse-level load metrics at regular time intervals?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.RESOURCE_USAGE_HISTORY

questions.explanation:
WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING provides 5-minute interval snapshots of each virtual warehouse's load state, including the number of queries currently RUNNING, QUEUED (waiting), and BLOCKED. This is the purpose-built view for identifying queuing pressure by warehouse and time. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY tracks credit consumption, not query load state. QUERY_HISTORY has per-query wait time data but requires aggregation to identify warehouse-level patterns. RESOURCE_USAGE_HISTORY is not a standard ACCOUNT_USAGE view name.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is preparing a monthly storage cost report. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides daily total storage usage broken down by AVERAGE_DATABASE_BYTES, AVERAGE_STAGE_BYTES, and AVERAGE_FAILSAFE_BYTES?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATABASE_STORAGE_USAGE_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY

questions.explanation:
ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE provides daily account-level snapshots with AVERAGE_DATABASE_BYTES (data in all databases), AVERAGE_STAGE_BYTES (data in Snowflake internal and external stages), and AVERAGE_FAILSAFE_BYTES (Fail-Safe storage). This view retains data for 1 year and is ideal for monthly storage cost reporting. TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS provides per-table storage but is a current point-in-time view, not historical. DATABASE_STORAGE_USAGE_HISTORY provides database-level history. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY tracks credit consumption, not storage bytes.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: An administrator needs to understand the credit cost of serverless Snowpipe ingestion and serverless task execution separately. Which TWO ACCOUNT_USAGE views provide this per-service breakdown? (Select TWO)

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY — for Snowpipe credit consumption per named pipe
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY — for serverless task credit consumption per task
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY — provides combined daily totals for all serverless services
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY — includes a SERVICE_TYPE column for Snowpipe and tasks

questions.explanation:
PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY provides per-pipe Snowpipe credit consumption, enabling cost attribution to individual ingestion pipelines. SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY provides per-task execution records with credit consumption, enabling drill-down into task-specific serverless costs. Together they provide granular attribution for the two main serverless services. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY (**C**) provides aggregate daily totals by service type but does not break down to individual pipes or tasks. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY (**D**) only captures virtual warehouse credits — it does not include serverless service costs.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview

questions.stem: An administrator runs GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA finance TO ROLE analyst_role followed by GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA finance TO ROLE analyst_role. Three tables already exist. A fourth table is created two weeks later. Which tables does analyst_role have SELECT on?

options.text: Only the three original tables — future grants only apply from the moment they are run, but the first command already covered them
All four tables — the first command covers the existing three and the future grant covers the new one
Only the fourth table — the ALL TABLES grant expires and the FUTURE grant takes over
All tables in the database, not just the finance schema

questions.explanation:
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA is a point-in-time grant that covers all tables that exist at the moment of execution — the three original tables. GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA creates a standing grant for all tables created in the schema going forward — covering the fourth table when it is created. Together, both commands provide complete coverage: existing tables from the ALL TABLES grant, future tables from the FUTURE TABLES grant. ALL TABLES grants do not expire. The grants are scoped to the specified schema, not the entire database.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: An administrator discovers that ANALYST_ROLE was accidentally granted INSERT privilege on a sensitive production table. Only SELECT should be allowed. Which is the most precise command to correct this?

options.text: REVOKE INSERT ON TABLE prod.secure.transactions FROM ROLE analyst_role;
REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES ON TABLE prod.secure.transactions FROM ROLE analyst_role;
GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE prod.secure.transactions TO ROLE sysadmin;
DROP ROLE analyst_role; CREATE ROLE analyst_role; -- Re-grant SELECT only

questions.explanation:
REVOKE INSERT ON TABLE removes only the INSERT privilege from ANALYST_ROLE on that specific table. The SELECT privilege remains intact. This is the most targeted, least-impact correction. REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES (**B**) would also revoke the SELECT privilege the role should keep. Granting OWNERSHIP to SYSADMIN (**C**) transfers the table's ownership, which may also revoke all existing grants — far beyond what is needed. Dropping and recreating the role (**D**) destroys all grants and memberships for the role.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A stored procedure PROCESS_HR_DATA runs with EXECUTE AS OWNER. The owner has elevated access to HR tables. To prevent misuse, the procedure should only execute sensitive operations when called by users with HR_ADMIN_ROLE in their hierarchy. Which function checks this?

options.text: CURRENT_ROLE() = 'HR_ADMIN_ROLE'
IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION('HR_ADMIN_ROLE')
IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE('HR_ADMIN_ROLE')
INVOKER_ROLE() = 'HR_ADMIN_ROLE'

questions.explanation:
IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE('role_name') is specifically designed for use inside EXECUTE AS OWNER stored procedures to check whether the caller (invoker) has a specific role granted anywhere in their role hierarchy — regardless of which role is currently active. This is the correct function for implementing caller-based access control inside owner's rights procedures. CURRENT_ROLE() returns the owner's active role (not the caller's role) when inside an EXECUTE AS OWNER procedure. IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() checks the current session's role context but inside EXECUTE AS OWNER this reflects the owner's session. INVOKER_ROLE() is not a valid Snowflake function.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A Snowflake role ETL_ROLE has been granted OWNERSHIP on a production schema. A security review identifies this as a risk. Which specific capability does OWNERSHIP on a schema grant that makes it risky for an ETL role?

options.text: The role can drop any table or view in the schema without requiring additional DROP privileges
The role can grant any privilege on any object within the schema to any other role, bypassing normal access control review
The role automatically gains ACCOUNTADMIN-level access when it owns a production schema
The role can modify the account-level time travel retention for all objects in the schema

questions.explanation:
OWNERSHIP of a schema allows the owning role to grant privileges on any object within that schema to any other role — including roles outside normal access control review processes. An ETL role with schema OWNERSHIP could escalate its own access or grant broad access to other roles, which is a privilege escalation risk.

Option **A** is partially true (owners can manage owned objects) but the primary security concern is the ability to grant privileges.

Option **C** is incorrect — owning a schema does not grant ACCOUNTADMIN.

Option **D** is incorrect — time travel retention at the account level cannot be modified by schema owners.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel

questions.stem: An account receives a new data share from a provider. The administrator needs to allow the DATA_ANALYSTS team role to query the shared data. The shared database is PARTNER_DB. Which privilege grant is specific to shared databases?

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE partner_db TO ROLE data_analysts;
GRANT IMPORTED PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE partner_db TO ROLE data_analysts;
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN DATABASE partner_db TO ROLE data_analysts;
GRANT READ ON SHARE partner_db TO ROLE data_analysts;

questions.explanation:
GRANT IMPORTED PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE is the privilege required for roles to access objects within a database created from an inbound data share. This single grant replaces the standard USAGE-on-database plus individual-object-grants pattern. Without IMPORTED PRIVILEGES, the role cannot access any objects in the shared database even with USAGE on the database. GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES works on local tables but not on shared databases. GRANT READ ON SHARE is not valid Snowflake syntax.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro

questions.stem: A data governance team wants to review all current privilege grants on a table FINANCE.REPORTING.BUDGET_SUMMARY, including who has SELECT, INSERT, and OWNERSHIP. Which command provides this information?

options.text: SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE finance.reporting.budget_summary;
SELECT * FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.OBJECT_PRIVILEGES WHERE TABLE_NAME = 'BUDGET_SUMMARY';
DESCRIBE TABLE finance.reporting.budget_summary PRIVILEGES;
SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE sysadmin ON TABLE finance.reporting.budget_summary;

questions.explanation:
SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE fully_qualified_table_name lists all privilege grants on that specific table, including the privilege type (SELECT, INSERT, OWNERSHIP, etc.), the grantee role name, and who granted each privilege (GRANTED_BY column). This is the definitive command for auditing object-level grants. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.OBJECT_PRIVILEGES can also provide this but has 7-day retention. DESCRIBE TABLE with PRIVILEGES is not valid syntax. SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE shows what a specific role has, not all grants on a specific object.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A Snowflake Native App installed in a consumer account defines Application Roles APP_VIEWER and APP_CONTRIBUTOR. Select TWO true statements about how these Application Roles work in the consumer account. (Select TWO)

options.text: Application Roles are granted to account roles in the consumer account — not directly to users
Application Roles can be listed with SHOW APPLICATION ROLES IN APPLICATION <app_name>
Application Roles are identical to regular account roles and appear in SHOW ROLES output
The consumer account can modify the privileges assigned to Application Roles

questions.explanation:
Application Roles are a Native App construct — the provider defines them and the consumer grants them to their account roles. In the consumer account, Application Roles are granted to account roles (not directly to users) using GRANT APPLICATION ROLE syntax. SHOW APPLICATION ROLES IN APPLICATION app_name lists the available Application Roles for a specific installed app. Application Roles do NOT appear in regular SHOW ROLES output — they are separate from account roles (option **C** is false). The consumer account cannot modify the privileges assigned to Application Roles — those are controlled entirely by the app provider (option **D** is false).

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/native-apps-about

questions.stem: A Snowflake account has a network policy applied at the account level. An administrator needs to apply a different, more permissive network policy to a single API service account user API_SVC. Which command applies a user-level network policy override?

options.text: ALTER USER api_svc SET NETWORK_POLICY = api_svc_policy;
ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY = api_svc_policy FOR USER api_svc;
GRANT NETWORK_POLICY api_svc_policy TO USER api_svc;
CREATE NETWORK POLICY OVERRIDE FOR USER api_svc USING api_svc_policy;

questions.explanation:
User-level network policies are assigned using ALTER USER user_name SET NETWORK_POLICY = policy_name. A policy set at the user level fully overrides the account-level policy for that specific user. The account-level policy continues to govern all other users. ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY with a FOR USER clause is not valid Snowflake syntax. GRANT NETWORK_POLICY and CREATE NETWORK POLICY OVERRIDE are not valid Snowflake commands.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: An administrator needs to review the IP allowlist and blocklist for a specific network policy named CORP_POLICY. Which command shows the details of this specific policy?

options.text: SHOW NETWORK POLICIES LIKE 'CORP_POLICY';
DESCRIBE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy;
SELECT * FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.NETWORK_POLICIES WHERE POLICY_NAME = 'CORP_POLICY';
Both A and B are valid ways to view network policy details

questions.explanation:
Both SHOW NETWORK POLICIES LIKE 'CORP_POLICY' and DESCRIBE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy provide details about the specific policy. SHOW NETWORK POLICIES LIKE filters the list to the matching policy and shows summary information including allowed and blocked IP list entry counts. DESCRIBE NETWORK POLICY provides the full detail of the policy including the complete ALLOWED_IP_LIST and BLOCKED_IP_LIST entries. INFORMATION_SCHEMA does not have a NETWORK_POLICIES view, so option **C** is invalid. Since both **A** and **B** are valid, option **D** is the correct answer.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator receives reports that an external stage backed by Google Cloud Storage (GCS) is returning permission errors. Which Snowflake function can be used to diagnose the connectivity and access permission issues with this external stage?

options.text: SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION('@my_gcs_stage')
SYSTEM$VALIDATE_GCS_INTEGRATION('my_gcs_integration')
LIST @my_gcs_stage — if it returns results, the stage connection is working
SELECT STAGE_STATUS FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STAGES WHERE STAGE_NAME = 'MY_GCS_STAGE';

questions.explanation:
SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION('@stage_name') is the Snowflake system function that tests connectivity to an external stage. It validates that Snowflake has the necessary permissions to list, read, and write to the underlying cloud storage location and returns diagnostic output indicating success or the specific failure reason. SYSTEM$VALIDATE_GCS_INTEGRATION is not a valid Snowflake function. LIST @stage will also show if listing works but does not provide detailed diagnostic output for failures. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STAGES does not have a real-time connectivity status column.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-load-local-file-system-create-stage

questions.stem: A company uses Snowflake on GCP with Private Service Connect enabled. They have an IP-based network policy with ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('10.128.0.0/20'). A client connecting via Private Service Connect from a GCP VPC with IP 10.200.1.5 (outside the allowed range) attempts to connect. What is the expected behavior?

options.text: Connection is blocked because 10.200.1.5 is not in the ALLOWED_IP_LIST
Connection succeeds because Private Service Connect traffic bypasses IP-based network policy enforcement
Connection fails, and the user must use a VPN to route traffic through the allowed IP range
Connection is blocked until the administrator explicitly enables Private Service Connect in the network policy

questions.explanation:
Similar to AWS PrivateLink, GCP Private Service Connect traffic arrives at Snowflake through the private network backbone without presenting a public routable IP address in the traditional sense for IP-based policy evaluation. As a result, standard IP-based network policies are bypassed for Private Service Connect connections. To enforce access controls for these connections, administrators should use the appropriate VPC-based network rule types rather than relying on IP address ranges. This is consistent behavior across cloud providers for private connectivity solutions.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: A security team wants to restrict Snowflake inbound connections to only specific AWS VPC endpoints. They plan to use network rules. Select TWO accurate statements about creating an AWSVPCEID network rule. (Select TWO)

options.text: The TYPE = AWSVPCEID network rule accepts VPC endpoint IDs (e.g., vpce-0123456789abcdef0) in the VALUE_LIST
AWSVPCEID rules are only available on Business Critical edition and above
An AWSVPCEID network rule must be included in a network policy to take effect
AWSVPCEID rules can be combined with TYPE = IPV4 rules in the same network policy to allow both VPC endpoint and IP-based access

questions.explanation:
TYPE = AWSVPCEID network rules accept VPC endpoint IDs (in the vpce-xxxxxxxx format) in the VALUE_LIST (option **A** is true). Like all network rules, AWSVPCEID rules must be referenced in a network policy (added to a network rule list in the policy) for the restriction to be enforced (option **C** is true). AWSVPCEID rules are not limited to Business Critical edition — they are available on standard Snowflake accounts with PrivateLink-capable deployments (option **B** is false). Different network rules of different types can indeed be combined within a network policy to allow multiple access paths (option **D** is partially true as a concept, but combining them in the same policy requires the correct policy structure).

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: A Snowflake table holds reference data that never changes. Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 on this table would save storage costs. What is the trade-off the administrator must accept?

options.text: The table cannot be queried after retention is set to 0
Time Travel is disabled; if data is accidentally modified or deleted, it cannot be recovered using Time Travel or AT/BEFORE queries
Setting retention to 0 also disables Fail-Safe, so the table has no recovery options whatsoever
The table is automatically converted to a TRANSIENT table type when retention is set to 0

questions.explanation:
Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 disables Time Travel for that table. This means AT/BEFORE queries cannot reference historical states, and accidental DML changes or drops cannot be recovered using Time Travel. The table itself remains fully queryable for current data. For permanent tables with retention 0, Fail-Safe still applies (7-day Snowflake-managed protection for permanent tables) — option **C** is incorrect. The table type does not change to TRANSIENT — it remains a permanent table. The trade-off is eliminating Time Travel recovery capability in exchange for reduced storage costs.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: A Snowflake developer needs a table that persists data between ETL pipeline stages (visible to multiple sessions and roles) but should NOT accumulate Fail-Safe storage costs since the data is easily regeneratable. Which table type is most appropriate?

options.text: PERMANENT table with DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0
TEMPORARY table with shared visibility across sessions
TRANSIENT table
EXTERNAL table pointing to an S3 stage

questions.explanation:
A TRANSIENT table is specifically designed for this use case: it persists across sessions and is visible to all roles (unlike temporary tables), but it has no Fail-Safe period (unlike permanent tables). Transient tables support 0 or 1 day of Time Travel only. This makes them cost-efficient for intermediate ETL data that is regeneratable and does not need long-term protection. A permanent table with retention 0 (**A**) still has a 7-day Fail-Safe period. TEMPORARY tables (**B**) are session-scoped and not visible to other sessions. EXTERNAL tables (**D**) are read-only and backed by external cloud storage.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: After running a large bulk insert operation, an administrator wants to check whether the DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS on the target table matches the schema-level setting. Which command shows this information for all tables in a schema?

options.text: SELECT TABLE_NAME, RETENTION_TIME FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES WHERE TABLE_SCHEMA = 'MY_SCHEMA';
DESCRIBE DATABASE mydb;
SHOW SCHEMAS IN DATABASE mydb;
SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS WHERE TABLE_SCHEMA = 'MY_SCHEMA';

questions.explanation:
INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES includes a RETENTION_TIME column that shows the effective DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS for each table. Filtering by TABLE_SCHEMA allows checking all tables in a specific schema. This is suitable for comparing table-level retention against the schema default. SHOW TABLES IN SCHEMA also shows a retention_time column in its output. DESCRIBE DATABASE shows database-level metadata, not table retention. SHOW SCHEMAS shows schema properties, not individual table retention. ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS shows storage volume per table, not retention configuration.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: A Snowflake warehouse is currently configured as STANDARD and the team wants to migrate memory-intensive Snowpark workloads to it without changing the existing warehouse name (other tools use the name). Which command changes the warehouse type in place?

options.text: ALTER WAREHOUSE my_wh SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED;
ALTER WAREHOUSE my_wh MODIFY TYPE SNOWPARK_OPTIMIZED;
GRANT SNOWPARK_OPTIMIZED_MEMORY ON WAREHOUSE my_wh TO ROLE sysadmin;
ALTER WAREHOUSE my_wh SET MEMORY_PER_CLUSTER_BYTES = MAXIMUM;

questions.explanation:
ALTER WAREHOUSE warehouse_name SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED is the supported syntax to convert an existing warehouse to the Snowpark-Optimized type without recreating it. This preserves the warehouse name and any existing parameter configurations. The type change applies on the next warehouse start cycle. MODIFY TYPE (**B**) is not valid Snowflake ALTER WAREHOUSE syntax. GRANT SNOWPARK_OPTIMIZED_MEMORY (**C**) is not a valid Snowflake privilege or command. SET MEMORY_PER_CLUSTER_BYTES (**D**) is not a valid warehouse parameter.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: An administrator queries ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS and finds TABLE_X with FAILSAFE_BYTES = 0 despite the account being on Enterprise edition. What is the most likely explanation?

options.text: TABLE_X has never had any data — it is empty
TABLE_X is a TRANSIENT or TEMPORARY table, which have no Fail-Safe period
FAILSAFE_BYTES accumulates slowly and has not had enough time to build up
The table owner disabled Fail-Safe using ALTER TABLE TABLE_X SET FAILSAFE = DISABLED

questions.explanation:
Transient and temporary tables have no Fail-Safe period in Snowflake by design. If TABLE_X is a TRANSIENT or TEMPORARY table, FAILSAFE_BYTES will always be 0 regardless of the Snowflake edition. This is one of the defining differences between permanent tables (7-day Fail-Safe on Enterprise) and transient/temporary tables (no Fail-Safe). An empty permanent table would show FAILSAFE_BYTES = 0 (**A**) but Enterprise permanent tables with data always accumulate Fail-Safe. Fail-Safe does not accumulate slowly — it is the same time window as the historical data being protected. Customers cannot disable Fail-Safe with ALTER TABLE (**D**).

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: A financial company needs to track all data transferred out of their Snowflake environment for GDPR egress compliance reporting. Specifically they need to know the volume of data (bytes) transferred to each destination region. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view satisfies this requirement?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY WHERE QUERY_TYPE = 'COPY'
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.COPY_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY

questions.explanation:
DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY in ACCOUNT_USAGE records all data transfer events, including BYTES_TRANSFERRED, SOURCE_CLOUD, SOURCE_REGION, TARGET_CLOUD, TARGET_REGION, and TRANSFER_TYPE. This view is specifically designed for egress monitoring and compliance reporting, allowing administrators to quantify data movement by destination region. QUERY_HISTORY captures query metadata but not structured transfer volume data. COPY_HISTORY tracks file-level COPY INTO operations focused on load status, not egress volume by region. PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY tracks Snowpipe ingestion (inbound), not egress.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-load-snowpipe-intro

questions.stem: A security team wants to identify all Snowflake sessions opened using outdated Python connector versions (below 3.0.0) to enforce connector upgrade policies. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view and column combination allows this filtering?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY — CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID column
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS — CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID column containing 'PythonConnector' and version
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY — CLIENT_DRIVER column
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS — CONNECTOR_VERSION column

questions.explanation:
ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS contains the CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID column which records the client application name and version for each session (e.g., 'PythonConnector 2.9.0'). By filtering SESSIONS WHERE CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID LIKE 'PythonConnector%' AND extracting the version number, administrators can identify sessions using outdated Python connector versions. The CLIENT_ENVIRONMENT column provides additional platform details. LOGIN_HISTORY (**A**) has CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID for login events but does not capture all session metadata. QUERY_HISTORY (**C**) has per-query client info but requires deduplication for per-session analysis. ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS (**D**) has no CONNECTOR_VERSION column.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts

questions.stem: An ORGADMIN needs to identify which accounts in the Snowflake organization have exceeded 10,000 credits consumed this month. Where can the ORGADMIN find cross-account credit consumption data?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY — query this from each account separately and aggregate
SNOWFLAKE.ORGANIZATION_USAGE.USAGE_IN_CURRENCY_DAILY — accessed from the org admin account with the ORGADMIN role
SNOWFLAKE.ORGANIZATION_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY — provides cross-account daily credit totals
Both B and C are valid views in ORGANIZATION_USAGE that provide cross-account credit data

questions.explanation:
The ORGANIZATION_USAGE schema (accessible to ORGADMIN) contains multiple views for cross-account visibility. USAGE_IN_CURRENCY_DAILY shows credit consumption converted to currency amounts by account, while METERING_DAILY_HISTORY in ORGANIZATION_USAGE provides credit consumption per account per service type. Both views enable identifying high-consumption accounts across the organization. ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY (**A**) is scoped to a single account and cannot aggregate across accounts. Since both B and C reference valid ORGANIZATION_USAGE views, option **D** is the most complete answer.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts
